clean_story:
Fake worlds. Real brain tricks.

"Virtual reality" means stealing your senses—just enough to make lies feel true. Your brain trusts what it sees, hears, and touches, but VR swaps real signals for digital ones, like a pickpocket replacing your wallet with an identical fake.

Here’s the hijack: VR headsets fire two slightly different images at your eyes (like your left and right hands holding a ball at arm’s length). Your brain, wired to merge mismatched views into depth, obeys—suddenly, flat screens become a 3D cliff. Add motion sensors: tilt your head, and the virtual sky tilts with you. Your inner ear expects this; no lag, no doubt. Now your balance system is complicit.

The coup de grâce? Haptic gloves. Press a virtual lemon, and tiny vibrators in the glove mimic texture—your fingers expect citrus oil. Studies show 78% of users flinch when a VR spider crawls on their hand, though nothing touches them. The brain fills gaps: if the illusion is seamless, it is reality.

Three related subjects are phantom limb pain, optical camouflage tricks, and how dreams fool senses.
